summary: DHH argues that the best New Year's resolution is to commit to improving competence at work, where you already spend 2,000 hours a year. Rather than forming new habits, he suggests dedicating even 10% of work time to deliberate practice and refinement. Using programming as his primary example, he advocates going beyond merely functional code to make it beautiful through clarity, cohesion, consistency, and conciseness. He emphasizes the pull request as a tool for self-collaboration and obsessive study of one's own work, arguing that time invested in refining technique pays dividends across a decades-long career.
key_points: Experience creates a trap: the more you've done things one way, the higher the perceived cost of trying something new, which is why paradigm shifts often come from outsiders

The standard 'make it work, make it right, make it fast' should be extended with 'make it beautiful' — pursuing aesthetic satisfaction forces you into unfamiliar techniques where real learning happens
Pull requests are primarily a tool for self-collaboration and studying your own work holistically, not just for team code review
Competence compounds over decades — time spent refining technique now pays back across a 20-60 year career, making 'I don't have time' a false economy
The write-revise loop is like gym reps: no single iteration matters, but relentless repetition over a year produces unmistakable results
money_quotes: Once you've been cooked in experience for too long, the cost of trying something new can seem prohibitive. But spend you must, if you aren't to stagnate.
When the bar isn't just a completed task but a delightful solution, you're frequently forced to find novel solutions in the wilderness of unfamiliar techniques.
Make it work, make it right, make it fast. And then, let me add one more: Make it beautiful.
Your career, and even your business, is not a single sprint. It's an intellectual iron man repeated until you're in the ground.
Whatever time you take to refine your technique now will be paid back to you for the next 20-40-60 years of your career.
Some people might see that primarily as a tool of collaboration with others, but I see it as a tool of collaboration with myself first.
The main blocker is your will to see it so.
key_insight: The most effective path to career growth is dedicating a portion of your existing work hours to deliberate practice — not just shipping functional work, but obsessively refining it toward beauty through clarity, cohesion, consistency, and conciseness.
